gso:Transform_Fault.definition_en_canonical: A variety of strike-slip fault along which the displacement suddenly stops or changes form, typically associated with mid-ocean ridges.
gso:Transform_Fault.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Wrench_Fault.definition_en_canonical: A strike slip fault in which the fault plane dips at least 45 degrees over more than half of the recognized extent of the fault.
gso:Wrench_Fault.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Fracture.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso.attribution: Brodaric & Richard 2021, GSC Open File 8796, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Anticline.definition_en_canonical: A fold whose core contains the stratigraphically older rocks
gso:Anticline.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Antiform.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Chevron_Fold.definition_en_canonical: A fold with straight limbs and sharp hinges. Well developed, these folds develop repeated set of v-shaped beds.  Inter-limb angles are generally 60 degrees or less. Chevron folding preferentially occurs in well bedded units where  bedding regularly alternates between contrasting competences.
gso:Chevron_Fold.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Fold.definition_en_canonical: A curve or bend of a planar structure, such as rock strata, bedding planes, foliation or cleavage. A fold is usually the product of deformation, involving the compression of strata, but may include primary structures, as its definition is descriptive, not genetic. (Neuendorf et al., 2011).
gso:Fold.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Fold_Amplitude.definition_en_canonical: the length of half the distance from the crest of a fold to the adjacent troughs.
gso:Fold_Amplitude.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Fold_Axial_Surface.definition_en_canonical: Abstract surface that bisects the limbs of a fold or is the normal to a surface defined by the poles to the folded surface (typically visualized on a stereonet).  The axial surface of a particular fold may be located based on observations of the folded geologic structure, but in general it has no direct physical manifestation. Contrast with hinge surface, which is defined as the surface containing a set of hinge lines in the stack of folded surfaces defining a fold structure.  As a geologic surface, the axial surface has geometric properties, including orientation, which may be specified by observations at one or more locations, or generalized using terminology (upright, inclined, reclined, recumbent, overturned). (revised from GeoSciML 3.2)
gso:Fold_Axial_Surface.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Fold_Hinge.definition_en_canonical: the line linking points of maximum curvature on a folded surface (http://www.geosci.usyd.edu.au/users/prey/Teaching/Geos-2123/Folds/Sld22.html). A low-dimensional feature
gso:Fold_Hinge.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Fold_Hinge_Surface.definition_en_canonical: Surface that contains the hinge lines in a stack of folded layers
gso:Fold_Hinge_Surface.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Fold_InterLimb_Angle.definition_en_canonical: The angle between adjacent limbs of a fold, normally determined by drawing tangents to the fold limbs at the inflection points.
gso:Fold_InterLimb_Angle.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Fold_Limb.definition_en_canonical: The generally planar region of a fold which lies between two adjacent hinge lines and is confined between the zones of maximum curvature. A role played by some layered rock reated to the adjacent fold hinges.  Not necessarily a feature
gso:Fold_Limb.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Fold_Limb_Shape.definition_en_canonical: Straight vs curved limbs, eg kink, chevron, sinusoidal, box
gso:Fold_Limb_Shape.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Fold_Span.definition_en_canonical: linear distance between inflection points in a single fold (GeoSciML v3.2)
gso:Fold_Span.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Fold_Symmetry.definition_en_canonical: Concordance or discordance of bisecting surface and axial surface, or ratio of length of limbs. Folded surface may have asymmetry defined by limb length ratio if inflection points are defined. Definition based on bisecting surface/axial surface angle depends on having multiple surfaces defined such that the axial surface may be identified. (symmetric, assymetric)
gso:Fold_Symmetry.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Fold_System.definition_en_canonical: A collection of congruent folds  (axis and axial surface are parallel) produced by the same tectonic event. Sometimes referred to as a \
gso:Fold_System.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Fold_Wavelength.definition_en_canonical: The distance between adjacent crest or trough of  a single fold, or the average distance between adjacent crests or troughs in a fold system.
gso:Fold_Wavelength.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Hinge_Line_Curvature.definition_en_canonical: Variation in orientation of fold hinge along trend of fold, distinguish sheath from cylindrical folds. Specify with terminology eg sheath, dome, basin, cylindrical.
gso:Hinge_Line_Curvature.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Hinge_Shape.definition_en_canonical: Rounded vs. angular hinge zones; has to do with the proportion of the wavelength that is considered part of hinge
gso:Hinge_Shape.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Is_Periodic.definition_en_canonical: boolean value, true if wavelength of folds in a fold system is approximately the same
gso:Is_Periodic.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Monocline.definition_en_canonical: step-like fold in rock strata consisting of a zone of steeper dip within an otherwise horizontal or gently-dipping sequence.
gso:Monocline.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Neutral_Fold.definition_en_canonical: A fold which closes laterally and is therefore neither antiformal nor synformal.
gso:Neutral_Fold.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Ptygmatic_Fold.definition_en_canonical: Chaotic and highly sinuous fold typically in an isolated layer within a more homogeneous rock body. Typical of sedimentary slump folding and veins or dikes in medium to high-grade metamorphic rocks
gso:Ptygmatic_Fold.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Recumbent.definition_en_canonical: Surface dips between 0 and 10 degrees; typically used to describe orientation of fold hing surface or axial surface, use equivalent term 'Horizontal inclination' for other geologic surfaces.
gso:Recumbent.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Recumbent_Fold.definition_en_canonical: A fold that has an essentially horizontal axial plane.
gso:Recumbent_Fold.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Syncline.definition_en_canonical: A fold of which the core contains the stratigraphically younger rocks
gso:Syncline.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Synform.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Upright_Inclination.definition_en_canonical: Surface dips between 80 and 90 degrees, used to describe orientation of fold hinge surface or axial surface;  use equivalent term 'Vertical inclination' for other geologic surfaces.
gso:Upright_Inclination.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:Vertical_Fold.definition_en_canonical: A fold for which the fold axis is vertical and axial surface is upright.
gso:Vertical_Fold.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso.attribution: Brodaric & Richard 2021, GSC Open File 8796, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:anastomosing_spaced_cleavage.definition_en_canonical: spaced cleavage domains branch and rejoin, creating lens-shaped microlithons.
gso:anastomosing_spaced_cleavage.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:bedding_fabric.definition_en_canonical: Fabric defined by textural or mineralogic variations in sediment produced by depositional processes. May be manifested by lamination structure within the sediment, or by layering in which the individual layers (beds) are bounded by discontinuities related to events in the depositional history.
sources: NADM sedimentary rock vocabulary (https://pubs.usgs.gov/of/2004/1451/sltt/appendixC/appendixC_pdf.zip)  SLTTs 2004, CGI foliationtype SKOS vocabulary 2018-08-03
gso:bedding_fabric.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:bedding_lamination_structure.definition_en_canonical: A surface internal to the containing sediment mass defined by textural or mineralogical variation, produced by sediment deposition process, and reflecting the orientation of the original depositional surface. Syn Laminated structure (NADM sedimentary rock vocabulary (https://pubs.usgs.gov/of/2004/1451/sltt/appendixC/appendixC_pdf.zip)  SLTTs 2004).
gso:bedding_lamination_structure.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:c_fabric.definition_en_canonical: Ductile shear banding foliation defined by spaced, discrete planar zones of high strain less than 1mm thick. Commonly associated with a particle flattening ('S') fabric to form a mylonitic foliation.
gso:c_fabric.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:cleavage.definition_en_canonical: A tectonic foliation in a rock characterized by a tendency for the rock to split along a regular set of parallel or sub-parallel closely spaced surfaces. Cleavage is a more general term than schistosity because schistosity need not be present in order for a rock to display cleavage. Cleavage domains or parting surfaces are not spaced more than 5 cm apart on average (Borradaille et al., 1982)
sources: Brodie, Kate, Fettes, Douglas, and Harte, Ben, 2007, Structural terms including fault rock terms, in Fettes, D., and Desmons, J., Metamorphic rocks-A classification and Glossary of Terms: Cambridge, UK, Cambridge University Press, p. 24-31.
gso:cleavage.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:cleavage_parallel_to_bedding.definition_en_canonical: Composite fabric in which a cleavage is developed parallel to bedding.
gso:cleavage_parallel_to_bedding.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:close_joints.definition_en_canonical: Foliation defined by parting surfaces (cleavage domains) that have no apparent thickness, are spaced between 5 cm and 25 cm, and do not crenulate an older foliation. Fabric intermediate between typical cleavage and joints. Parting surfaces are regularly spaced, and penetrative on a 1-10 m scale. 5 cm upper limit on spacing of 'cleavage domains' in a 'cleavage 'fabric is suggested by Borradaile et al. [1982].
sources: Borradaile et al. 1982, CGI foliationtype SKOS vocabulary 2018-08-03
gso:close_joints.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:comb_layering.definition_en_canonical: Layering fabric defined by layers that have internal comb structure. Layer boundaries are the termination surfaces for the constituent crystals.
sources: Neuendorf et al. 2005, CGI foliationtype SKOS vocabulary 2018-08-03
gso:comb_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:compositional_layering.definition_en_canonical: Layering fabric defined by layers that have different mineralogic composition.
sources: CGI foliationtype SKOS vocabulary 2018-08-03, submitted by British Antarctic Survey
gso:compositional_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:compound_foliation.definition_en_canonical: Foliation that includes fabric elements indicating classification with foliation types of different genetic origin, e.g. cleavage parallel to bedding is a compound fabric with a sedimentary foliation and a tectonic foliation. No connotation of genetic origin of fabric elements.
gso:compound_foliation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:compound_metamorphic_foliation.definition_en_canonical: A compound foliation in which the constituent fabric elements are of metamorphic origin.
gso:compound_metamorphic_foliation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:continuous_cleavage.definition_en_canonical: A cleavage that is statistically homogeneous down to the scale of individual mineral grains.
gso:continuous_cleavage.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:continuous_crenulation_cleavage.definition_en_canonical: Cleavage defined by long, thin limbs of crenulation folds of pre-existing foliation, in which the boundaries between cleavage domains and lithons are gradational. Both the crenulation foliation and the crenulated (older) foliation are easily visible. Grades to spaced crenulation cleavage as boundaries of cleavage domains become sharp, and to schistosity or compositional layering as the crenulation foliation obscures the crenulated foliation to become the dominant fabric in the rock. In many rocks with continuous crenulation cleavage, the tendency to part along the cleavage may be weak, thus the rock may not technically meet the definition of 'cleavage as used in CGI foliationtype SKOS vocabulary 2018-08-03.
gso:continuous_crenulation_cleavage.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:crenulation_cleavage.definition_en_canonical: Cleavage that is overprinted on an older foliation that is folded to some degree in association with development of the younger cleavage. Cleavage domains commonly associated with long limbs of crenulation folds, and hinge surfaces of folds are roughly parallel. Wavelength of crenulation folds is less than or equal to 1 cm. Cleavage defined by limbs of microfolds coincident with zones of mineral differentiation
sources: Brodie, Kate, Fettes, Douglas, and Harte, Ben, 2007, Structural terms including fault rock terms, in Fettes, D., and Desmons, J., Metamorphic rocks-A classification and Glossary of Terms: Cambridge, UK, Cambridge University Press, p. 24-31.
gso:crenulation_cleavage.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:crude_or_indistinct_bedding.definition_en_canonical: Bedding defined by surfaces that are not distinct, e.g. by gradational grain size variations.
gso:crude_or_indistinct_bedding.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:cryptomicrobial_lamination.definition_en_canonical: Fabric defined by fine laminae formed by the trapping of grains and lime precipitation in a microbial mat.
gso:cryptomicrobial_lamination.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:cumulate_layering.definition_en_canonical: Layering in igneous rocks in which layers are characterized by variation in relative proportion of magmatically crystallized minerals. Typically a pattern of mineralogical variation will be repeated many times in a vertical section with layers of relatively consistent thickness. See photos in Best [1982] p. 176-177. Gravity-stratified layers typically have mafic minerals at the base and plagioclase at the top and are interpreted to form by crystal settling in magma.
gso:cumulate_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:discrete_cleavage.definition_en_canonical: Spaced cleavage in which cleavage domains have sharply defined edges.
gso:discrete_cleavage.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:discrete_crenulation_cleavage.definition_en_canonical: Crenulation cleavage in which the cleavage domains have discrete boundaries. Amplitude of crenulation folds tends to be small.
gso:discrete_crenulation_cleavage.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:discrete_disjunct_spaced_cleavage.definition_en_canonical: Disjunct spaced cleavage defined by discrete cleavage surfaces, typically in low-grade clastic rocks. Syn. Fracture cleavage.
sources: Neuendorf et al. 2005, Borradaile et al. 1982.
gso:discrete_disjunct_spaced_cleavage.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:disjunctive_cleavage.definition_en_canonical: A spaced cleavage independent of any pre-existing mineral orientation, cleavage in which there is no systematic relationship between a pre-existing preferred orientation and the superimposed cleavage. (Borradaile et al., 1982)
gso:disjunctive_cleavage.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:domainal_cleavage.definition_en_canonical: Fabric in which domains of more strongly developed (closely spaced) cleavage are separated by lenses of weakly or non-cleaved rock. Typically observed on outcrop scale and used for geologic unit description
gso:domainal_cleavage.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:ductile_shear_banding_foliation.definition_en_canonical: Foliation defined by regularly spaced ductile shear bands at the scale of descripiton.
gso:ductile_shear_banding_foliation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:eutaxitic_foliation.definition_en_canonical: Foliation defined by flattened pumice clasts or glass shards, aligned elongate lithic fragments, variations in composition, vesicularity, crystallinity, grain size, spherulite and lithophysae abundance, or the degree of devitrification in a welded tuff. This term denotes interpretation that foliation formed during compaction and welding of tuff.
sources: Neuendorf et al. 2005, CGI foliationtype SKOS vocabulary 2018-08-03
gso:eutaxitic_foliation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:fissile_lamination.definition_en_canonical: Diffuse to distinct lamination in mudstones and mud, defined by the alignment of clay minerals. Lacks distinct very-fine scale layering and repetition of couplets that characterize varves. Essentially a continuous cleavage parallel to bedding, but considered a sedimentary structure related to sediment compaction during diagenesis.
sources: NADM sedimentary rock vocabulary (https://pubs.usgs.gov/of/2004/1451/sltt/appendixC/appendixC_pdf.zip)  SLTTs 2004, CGI foliationtype SKOS vocabulary 2018-08-03
gso:fissile_lamination.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:flaser_structure.definition_en_canonical: Tectonic foliation defined by lenses and layers of original or relatively unaltered granular minerals surrounded by a matrix of highly sheared and crushed material. No connotation of metamorphic grade.
gso:flaser_structure.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:flow_layering.definition_en_canonical: Layering produced by flow of magma, lava, or diapiric flow of sedimentary rock. Distinction from eutaxitic foliation may be difficult.
gso:flow_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:generic_mylonitic_foliation.definition_en_canonical: Foliation defined by the shapes of deformed mineral grains or grain aggregates having aspect ratios greater than 1.5:1, greater than 10 percent of the rock is composed of 'matrix' showing evidence of tectonic reduction in grain size, and the foliation and matrix are interpreted to be the product of continuous, crystal-plastic deformation processes. Generic class is used when data are insufficient to determine classification to protomylontic, mylonite, or ultramylonitc foliation, or to specify that any sort of mylontic foliation may be present.
gso:generic_mylonitic_foliation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:gneissic_layering.definition_en_canonical: Compositional layering in a metamorphic rock, syn. gneissic banding
gso:gneissic_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:grain_shape_foliation.definition_en_canonical: Foliation defined by the alignment of oblate particles, which may be detrital grains, crystals, or crystalline grain aggregates.
gso:grain_shape_foliation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:igneous_flow_foliation.definition_en_canonical: Foliation interpreted to be due to flow in a body of magma or lava. Includes flow banding in lava, and foliation defined by aligned crystals in a phaneritic igneous rock..
gso:igneous_flow_foliation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:igneous_lamination.definition_en_canonical: Foliation defined by surfaces of textural or mineralogical variation formed by igneous processes. If the lamination surfaces are regularly spaced, they may be used to define layers.
gso:igneous_lamination.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:igneous_layering.definition_en_canonical: Layering that is the product of processes operating in magma or lava. syn. magmatic layering
gso:igneous_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:igneous_textural_layering.definition_en_canonical: Layering defined by grain size/ grain shape variations resulting from crystallization processes in igneous rock, most commonly observed in pegmatite/aplite dikes or bodies, and in Laramide leucogranites of Wilderness suite in Arizona.
gso:igneous_textural_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:imbricated_clast_fabric.definition_en_canonical: Fabric defined by long axis of tabular clasts stacked like shingles to define a surface that makes a small angle with the bedding surface. A depositional fabric formed by disk-shaped or elongate pebbles all tilted in the same direction, flat sides commonly dip upstream
gso:imbricated_clast_fabric.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:impersistent_lamination.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:irregular_lamination.definition_en_canonical: Lamination fabric in which individual lamina or groups of lamina are warped in an irregular fashion, like a flattened peice of crumpled paper. A crude planar orientation is discernible.
gso:irregular_lamination.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:joint_fabric.definition_en_canonical: Fabric defined by parting surfaces in rock that occur in crudely parallel orientation, spaced greater than 5 cm on average if in a very regular joint set. Spacing is more variable and alignment of surfaces is cruder that cleavage. Typically applies to geologic unit description.
gso:joint_fabric.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:laminated_metamorphic_layering.definition_en_canonical: Metamorphic layering in which the layers are less than 1 cm thick. See Weiss, L.E. [1972] plate 36 or Borraidaile et al [1982] plate 137A B (page 331), plate140A (page 337) for examples.
sources: Weiss 1972, Borradaile et al 1982, CGI foliationtype SKOS vocabulary 2018-08-03
gso:laminated_metamorphic_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:lava_flow_banding.definition_en_canonical: Layering formed by laminar lava flow, defined by variations in composition, vesicularity, crystallinity, grainsize, spherulite and lithophysae abundance, degree of devitrification, colour, and/or parting surfaces, syn. flow lamination, flow banding
gso:lava_flow_banding.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:layered_mylonitic_foliation.definition_en_canonical: Compound foliation in which mylonitic foliation is present in rock that has continuous compositional layering. Continuous means individual layers can be traced more than 1 m.
gso:layered_mylonitic_foliation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:layering.definition_en_canonical: Foliation defined by stacked, differentiable, sheet-like masses of material (layers), with no denotation of the nature (composition, grain size, grain shape...) or origin (igneous, sedimentary, metamorphic) of the layers.
gso:layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:low_temperature_flow_foliation.definition_en_canonical: Foliation interpreted to be due to flow in a body of rock (typically mudrock, salt, or anhydrite) under low-temperature (non-metamorphic) conditions, typically related to diapirism.
gso:low_temperature_flow_foliation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:metamorphic_differentiation_layering.definition_en_canonical: Layering defined by mineralogical variations in a rock body interpreted to be result of solid-state reactions in rock body (metamorphic segregation).
gso:metamorphic_differentiation_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:metamorphic_layering.definition_en_canonical: Layering of unspecified origin in a metamorphic rock. Compositional or textural variations may be inherited from protolith, or result from metamorphic processes.
gso:metamorphic_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:metamorphic_layering_inherited_from_.definition_en_canonical: Metamorphic layering in which layer differntiation is relict of compositional variations in the protolith, typically heterogeneous sedimentary or volcanic rock.
gso:metamorphic_layering_inherited_from_.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:mineralogical_layering.definition_en_canonical: Generic layering defined by variations in mineralogy between individual sheets of material
gso:mineralogical_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:mylonite_foliation.definition_en_canonical: Generic mylonitic foliation in which 50-90 percent of rock is matrix due to tectonic grain size reduction processes. Planar fabric is very apparent due to flattening of mineral grains, development of shear surfaces, and alignment of tabular mineral grains. Lineation is typically present and easily observed.
sources: NADM metamorphic rock vocabulary (https://pubs.usgs.gov/of/2004/1451/sltt/appendixB/appendixB.pdf) SLTTm1.0 2004,  Sibson 1977
gso:mylonite_foliation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:parallel_bedding.definition_en_canonical: Bedding defined by parallel surfaces. Bedding structure may be lamination or layering.
sources: NADM sedimentary rock vocabulary NADM sedimentary rock vocabulary (https://pubs.usgs.gov/of/2004/1451/sltt/appendixC/appendixC_pdf.zip)  SLTTs 2004
gso:parallel_bedding.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:parallel_bedding_lamination.definition_en_canonical: Bedding lamination structure in which all visible bedding surfaces are parallel.
gso:parallel_bedding_lamination.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:phyllitic_cleavage.definition_en_canonical: Continuous cleavage in phyllitic rock, phyllosilicate mineral grains just barely visible, grain size midway between slaty cleavage and schist, typically has a satiny lustre.
sources: NADM metamorphic rock vocabulary (https://pubs.usgs.gov/of/2004/1451/sltt/appendixB/appendixB.pdf) SLTTm1.0 2004,  Geoscience Victoria, Australia
gso:phyllitic_cleavage.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:planar_lamination.definition_en_canonical: Lamination that is defined by planar bedding surfaces at the scale of description.
gso:planar_lamination.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:primary_foliation.definition_en_canonical: Foliation formed during original process of deposition or crystallization of an igneous or sedimentary material.
gso:primary_foliation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:primary_igneous_foliation.definition_en_canonical: Foliation interpreted to be the product of igneous processes, formed in the rock while melt was still present..
gso:primary_igneous_foliation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:protomylonite_foliation.definition_en_canonical: Generic mylonitic foliation in which 10-50 percent of rock is matrix due to tectonic grain size reduction processes. Planar aspect of fabric may be quite subtle in weakly deformed rocks.
sources: NADM metamorphic rock vocabulary (https://pubs.usgs.gov/of/2004/1451/sltt/appendixB/appendixB.pdf) SLTTm1.0 2004,  Sibson 1977
gso:protomylonite_foliation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:rhythmic_layering.definition_en_canonical: Layering in igneous rock defined by repeated gravity-stratified layers of typically mafic minerals at the base and plagioclase at the top, formed by crystal settling
gso:rhythmic_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:rough_cleavage.definition_en_canonical: Disjunct (Borradaile et al, 1990) cleavage characterized by short, discontinuous clavage surfaces, typically with concentrations of phyllosilicate minerals. Common in micaceous sandstone.
sources: Neuendorf et al. 2005, Borradaile et al, 1990
gso:rough_cleavage.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:s_fabric.definition_en_canonical: Foliation in mylonite defined by alignment of tabular crystals or deformed mineral grains, often with sigmoid shape, typically curving into an associated ductile shear banding foliation (C fabric) with a consistent sense.
gso:s_fabric.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:scaly_cleavage.definition_en_canonical: Discrete, anastomosing spaced cleavage characterized by smooth and highly polished/slickensided cleavage surfaces that intersect with one another to form lensoid flakes, scales or chips. Common in pelitic rocks in shear zones, melange, or deformed accretionary complexes.
gso:scaly_cleavage.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:schistosity.definition_en_canonical: A fabric defined the parallel, planar arrangement of mineral grains having a platy, lamellar, or tabular crystallographic habit that are oriented in a continuous planar or rarely a linear fabric. Schistosity is commonly, but not necessarily, a crystalloblastic foliation. Platy mineral grains typically 1 to 10 mm across (Jackson, 1997), at smaller grain size grades into phyllitic cleavage. Schistosity is typically associated with a continuous cleavage due to the tendency to part parallel to the aligned mineral grains.
gso:schistosity.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:sedimentary_layering.definition_en_canonical: Layering resulting from primary sedimentary processes, in which the individual layers (beds) are bounded by discontinuities related to events in the depositional history.
gso:sedimentary_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:slaty_cleavage.definition_en_canonical: Continuous cleavage defined by aligned fine to very-fine grained phylosilicate mineral grains. The individual grains defining the fabric are too small to be seen by the unaided eye. Grades into phyllitic cleavage as grain size increases. Cleavage domains typically less than 100 micrometres wide, rock easily splits into slabs and thin plates.
sources: Brodie, Kate, Fettes, Douglas, and Harte, Ben, 2007, Structural terms including fault rock terms, in Fettes, D., and Desmons, J., Metamorphic rocks-A classification and Glossary of Terms: Cambridge, UK, Cambridge University Press, p. 24-31., Jackson 1997
gso:slaty_cleavage.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:solution_cleavage.definition_en_canonical: Spaced cleavage forming an array of parallel to anastomosing, stylolitic to smooth, fracture-like partings formed by rock dissolution. Dissolution is indicated by discontinuity and offset of primary features that intersect cleavage surfaces, and typically by accumulation of insoluble residue along cleavage surface. Syn. stylolitic cleavage, pressure solution cleavage
gso:solution_cleavage.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:spaced_cleavage.definition_en_canonical: Cleavage in which cleavage domains are spaced at finite intervals as the scale of description. Microlithons of uncleaved rock separate cleavage domains.
gso:spaced_cleavage.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:stromatic_layering.definition_en_canonical: Tectonic layering in which variations that distinguish the layers are the result of melt segregation during high-grade metamorphism, some layers have magmatic/igneous mineralogy. Plates 143 and 144, Borradaile et al [1982] (page 344-347) Syn. Migmatitic layering, lit-par-lit layering
sources: Weiss 1972, Borradaile et al 1982, Neuendorf et al. 2005
gso:stromatic_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:stromatolitic_lamination.definition_en_canonical: Lamination produced by sediment trapping, binding and or precipitation by cyanophytes (blue-green algae)
gso:stromatolitic_lamination.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:tectonic_foliation.definition_en_canonical: Foliation in a rock body defined by physical components related to deformation subsequent to solidification of the rock.
gso:tectonic_foliation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:tectonic_layering.definition_en_canonical: Layering defined by mineralogical or textural variations in a rock body that are interpreted to be related to deformation or metamorphism, commonly associated with layers that have sharp boundaries. Also known as gneissic foliation. See plates 142-145 (p. 342-349) in Borradaile et al [1982] and plate 46A to 49A in Weiss [1972].
sources: Weiss 1972, Borradaile et al 1982, CGI foliationtype SKOS vocabulary 2018-08-03
gso:tectonic_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:transposed_bedding_layering.definition_en_canonical: Layering interpreted to have originated as sedimentary bedding that has been transposed as a result of metamorphism and deformation.
gso:transposed_bedding_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:ultramylonite_foliation.definition_en_canonical: Mylonitic foliation in which greater than 90 percent of rock is matrix due to tectonic grain size reduction processes. Typically rock is very-fine grained to aphanitic, with sparse porphyroclasts, and vague to prominent lamination on a mm scale.
sources: NADM metamorphic rock vocabulary (https://pubs.usgs.gov/of/2004/1451/sltt/appendixB/appendixB.pdf) SLTTm1.0 2004,  Sibson 1977
gso:ultramylonite_foliation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:undulating_lamination.definition_en_canonical: Gently curviplanar or wavy bedding lamination structure
gso:undulating_lamination.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:vague_metamorphic_layering.definition_en_canonical: Metamorphic layering of uncertain origin, defined by subtle mineralogical or textural variations, with gradational layer boundaries. See plate 142 in Borradaile et al [1982].
sources: Weiss 1972,Borradaile et al 1982, CGI foliationtype SKOS vocabulary 2018-08-03
gso:vague_metamorphic_layering.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:varve_lamination.definition_en_canonical: Bedding defined by distinct, parallel laminae (layers less than 1 cm thick), typically occurring in sequences of distinct couplets (e.g. dark and light color). Generally interpreted as deposited from suspension in still water, with each lamina thought to be an annual sedimentation unit. Typically consists of mud-size sediment.
sources: NADM sedimentary rock vocabulary (https://pubs.usgs.gov/of/2004/1451/sltt/appendixC/appendixC_pdf.zip)  NADM sedimentary rock vocabulary (https://pubs.usgs.gov/of/2004/1451/sltt/appendixC/appendixC_pdf.zip)  SLTTs 2004, Jackson 1997
gso:varve_lamination.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso.attribution: Brodaric & Richard 2021, GSC Open File 8796, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:augen_lineation.definition_en_canonical: A shape lineation defined by elongate augen.
gso:augen_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:bedding_cleavage_intersection_lineat.definition_en_canonical: Lineation defined by the intersection of bedding surfaces and cleavage surfaces.
gso:bedding_cleavage_intersection_lineat.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:biotite_mineral_lineation.definition_en_canonical: Mineral lineation defined by aligned elongate biotite aggregates in a foliation surface. Biotite does not have an elongate (prismatic, acicular) mineral habit, so production of lineation must reflect elongate aggregates of biotite crystals.
gso:biotite_mineral_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:cleavage_intersection_lineation.definition_en_canonical: Lineation defined by the intersection of two cleavages.
gso:cleavage_intersection_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:crenulation_lineation.definition_en_canonical: Lineation defined by small-scale wrinkles, typically of phylosillicate minerals, on another surface, typically a cleavage or schistosity. Use instead of intersection lineation if the wrinkles are the measured feature and the intersecting planar feature is not apparent.
gso:crenulation_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:flow_lineation.definition_en_canonical: A linear alignment of fabric elements, especially elongate or rod-shaped minerals, interpreted to have been aligned by flow of magma, lava, pyroclastic material, or diapiric flow of sedimentary rock.
gso:flow_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:hornblende_mineral_lineation.definition_en_canonical: Mineral lineation defined by aligned hornblende crystals.
gso:hornblende_mineral_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:igneous_lineation.definition_en_canonical: Lineation defined by alignment of elongate fabric elements formed in lava or magma before complete crystallization. Some typical fabric elements are elongate phenocrysts and vesicles.
gso:igneous_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:igneous_mineral_lineation.definition_en_canonical: Lineation in igneous rock defined by aligned crystals where there is evidence that the crystal alignment is related to process occurring before the rock was solidified.
gso:igneous_mineral_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:igneous_particle_shape_lineation.definition_en_canonical: Lineation in an igneous rock, usually welded tuff, defined by elongate objects in the rock (usually pumice pads, also elongate crystals). Descriptive--elogation may be due to deformation or to rotation/alignment of originally elongate (prolate ellipsoid) objects in the rock.
gso:igneous_particle_shape_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:igneous_vesicle_lineation.definition_en_canonical: lineation defined in a volcanic rock by aligned elongation direction of vesicles.
gso:igneous_vesicle_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:intersection_lineation.definition_en_canonical: A lineation produced by the intersection of two planar fabrics.
sources: Neuendorf et al 2005, CGI lineationtype SKOS vocabulary 2016-11-21
gso:intersection_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:mineral_lineation.definition_en_canonical: Lineation defined by alignment of elongate crystals that have a prismatic crystal habit or of monomineralic grain aggregates.
sources: Neuendorf et al 2005, CGI lineationtype SKOS vocabulary 2016-11-21
gso:mineral_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:particle_shape_lineation.definition_en_canonical: Lineation defined by alignment of long axes of prolate-shaped particles such as clasts, fossils, crystals, or phenocrysts. Particles may be polycrystalline aggregates formed by deformation of pre-existing particles. General category does not specify genesis of particles or their alignment.
gso:particle_shape_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:pencil_lineation.definition_en_canonical: Lineation defined by tendency of rock to break into long, slender pieces resembling pencils. Typically produced by intesection of bedding and cleavage or intersection of two cleavages. Fabric is generally only observed in weakly metamorphosed rocks.
gso:pencil_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:quartz_fibre_lineation.definition_en_canonical: Aligned quartz fibres that define a measurable lineation
gso:quartz_fibre_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:rodding_lineation.definition_en_canonical: Linear structure in metamorphic rocks in which the stronger parts have been shaped into parallel rods (eg. vein quartz)
gso:rodding_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:sillimanite_mineral_lineation.definition_en_canonical: Mineral lineation defined by aligned sillimanite crystals.
gso:sillimanite_mineral_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:slickenline.definition_en_canonical: Lineation on a brittle slip surface defined by grooves, ridges, or striations
gso:slickenline.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:stretching_lineation.definition_en_canonical: Lineation defined by elongate (prolate) fabric elements in which the alignment of the fabric elements, and the formation of prolate fabric elements is interpreted to be the product of crystal plastic deformation processes. Associated with mylontic foliation.
gso:stretching_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

gso:tectonic_lineation.definition_en_canonical: Penetrative linear structure in a rock body defined by fabric elements that are the product of tectonic processes. Linear is used in the sense that the orientation of the structure can be represented as a line in some sufficiently small neighborhood of any point in the material containig the fabric. Penetrative denotes that the fabric elements defining the structure are repeated at distances so small, compared with the scale of the whole that they can be considered to pervade it uniformly and be present at every point (paraphrase of Turner and Weiss, 1963, p. 21).
gso:tectonic_lineation.attribution: GSO v1.0 (Brodaric & Richard 2021), GSC OF 8796, CC BY 4.0, DOI 10.4095/328296

meta.description: Geolytics Seismic Module — consolidated acquisition, processing, inversion and attributes
sources: Yilmaz, O. (2001). Seismic Data Analysis. SEG Investigations in Geophysics No. 10.
Sheriff, R.E. & Geldart, L.P. (1995). Exploration Seismology. Cambridge University Press.
Russell, B.H. (1988). Introduction to Seismic Inversion Methods. SEG Course Notes.
Connolly, P. (1999). Elastic Impedance. The Leading Edge, 18(4), 438-452.
Chopra, S. & Marfurt, K.J. (2007). Seismic Attributes. SEG.
Coleou, T. et al. (2003). Unsupervised seismic facies classification. The Leading Edge.
OSDU SeismicAcquisitionSurvey / SeismicProcessingProject / SeismicTraceData schemas v1.0.0

modules.label: Seismic Acquisition
modules.description: Classes, properties, relations and instances covering seismic survey acquisition: survey types, sources, receivers, geometry and sampling parameters.
sources: Yilmaz, O. (2001). Seismic Data Analysis. SEG Investigations in Geophysics No. 10.
Sheriff, R.E. & Geldart, L.P. (1995). Exploration Seismology (2nd ed.). Cambridge University Press.

modules.label: Seismic Processing
modules.description: Classes, properties, relations and instances for the seismic processing workflow: noise suppression, deconvolution, velocity analysis, statics, NMO, DMO, migration, stacking and amplitude compensation.
sources: Yilmaz, O. (2001). Seismic Data Analysis. SEG Investigations in Geophysics No. 10.
Sheriff, R.E. & Geldart, L.P. (1995). Exploration Seismology (2nd ed.). Cambridge University Press.

modules.label: Seismic Inversion and Attributes
modules.description: Classes, properties, relations and instances for seismic inversion (post-stack, pre-stack, AVO, FWI), rock properties derived from inversion, seismic attributes, DHI classification, AVO anomaly classes, machine-learning facies interpretation (SOM, PCA) and sweet-spot mapping.
sources: Yilmaz, O. (2001). Seismic Data Analysis. SEG Investigations in Geophysics No. 10.
Russell, B.H. (1988). Introduction to Seismic Inversion Methods. SEG Course Notes.
Connolly, P. (1999). Elastic Impedance. The Leading Edge, 18(4), 438-452.
Chopra, S. & Marfurt, K.J. (2007). Seismic Attributes for Stratigraphic and Structural Interpretation. SEG.
Coleou, T., Poupon, M. & Azbel, K. (2003). Unsupervised seismic facies classification. The Leading Edge, 22(10), 942-953.

SeismicSurvey.name: SeismicSurvey
SeismicSurvey.description: A controlled experiment in which seismic energy is injected into the Earth and the returning wavefield is recorded to image subsurface structure. Encompasses geometry definition, source specification and receiver deployment. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 1.

LandSurvey.name: LandSurvey
LandSurvey.description: Onshore seismic survey in which sources (vibroseis, dynamite) and receivers (geophones) are deployed on land. Near-surface weathering layer and statics corrections are dominant challenges. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 3.

MarineSurvey.name: MarineSurvey
MarineSurvey.description: Offshore towed-streamer survey in which a vessel tows one or more hydrophone cables. Dominant configuration for deep-water exploration. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 1; Sheriff & Geldart 1995.

OBNSurvey.name: OBNSurvey
OBNSurvey.description: Ocean-Bottom Node survey: autonomous 4C (Vx, Vy, Vz + hydrophone) sensor packages placed on the seabed. Enables full-azimuth illumination and longer offsets than towed streamers. Ref: Sheriff & Geldart 1995.

OBSSurvey.name: OBSSurvey
OBSSurvey.description: Ocean-Bottom Seismometer/Cable survey: 4C sensors deployed on cables laid on the sea floor, offering P- and S-wave recording for joint PP/PS inversion.

Source.name: Source
Source.description: Device or process that generates seismic energy and injects it into the ground or water. Characterised by peak frequency, dominant frequency band and energy output. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 2.

Airgun.name: Airgun
Airgun.description: Marine seismic source that releases compressed air (typically 2000 psi) into the water to generate an impulsive pressure wavelet. Standard deep-water acquisition source. Peak frequency range 10-100 Hz. Ref: Sheriff & Geldart 1995.

Vibroseis.name: Vibroseis
Vibroseis.description: Land seismic source that emits a swept-frequency (chirp) signal via a vibrating baseplate hydraulically coupled to the ground. Allows controlled bandwidth 6-120 Hz. Requires cross-correlation with pilot sweep to produce zero-phase wavelet. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 2.

Dynamite.name: Dynamite
Dynamite.description: Explosive seismic source detonated in a shallow drill hole to generate a broad-band (5-200 Hz) near-impulsive wavelet. Preferred in areas of complex near-surface topography where vibroseis coupling is poor. Ref: Sheriff & Geldart 1995.

SparkerArray.name: SparkerArray
SparkerArray.description: High-resolution marine source using electrical discharge to vaporise water and generate a broadband (50-1000 Hz) pressure pulse. Used in ultra-high-resolution shallow surveys. Ref: Sheriff & Geldart 1995.

Receiver.name: Receiver
Receiver.description: Sensor that converts mechanical ground or water motion into an electrical signal. Sensitivity, natural frequency and dynamic range determine the recordable bandwidth. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 2.

Hydrophone.name: Hydrophone
Hydrophone.description: Pressure-sensitive piezoelectric sensor towed in marine streamers. Records scalar pressure wavefield (P-wave only). Sensitivity ~10 mV/Pa. Ref: Sheriff & Geldart 1995.

Geophone.name: Geophone
Geophone.description: Velocity-sensitive electromagnetic sensor planted on the ground surface. Natural frequency typically 10 Hz (land) or 4.5 Hz (OBC). Records particle velocity. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 2.

AccelerometerMEMS.name: AccelerometerMEMS
AccelerometerMEMS.description: Micro-Electro-Mechanical System accelerometer used in nodal acquisition systems. Flat response from DC to several hundred Hz; measures particle acceleration. Superior low-frequency response compared to geophone. Ref: Sheriff & Geldart 1995.

OBC.name: OBC
OBC.description: Ocean-Bottom Cable: multi-component cable laid on the sea floor carrying collocated hydrophones and geophones (4C). Enables dual-sensor summation and S-wave recording. Ref: Sheriff & Geldart 1995.

AcquisitionGeometry.name: AcquisitionGeometry
AcquisitionGeometry.description: Spatial arrangement of sources and receivers defining illumination, fold, offset distribution and azimuth coverage. Critical for migration operator accuracy. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 1.

StreamerLayout.name: StreamerLayout
StreamerLayout.description: Marine geometry in which one or more hydrophone cables (streamers) are towed behind a vessel. Characterised by streamer length, cable separation, tow depth and number of streamers.

NodalLayout.name: NodalLayout
NodalLayout.description: Land or OBN geometry employing autonomous wireless nodes deployed in a regular or irregular grid. Enables full-azimuth illumination.

SamplingParameters.name: SamplingParameters
SamplingParameters.description: Temporal and spatial sampling parameters that define the recorded wavefield fidelity and alias constraints. Must satisfy the Nyquist theorem in both time and space. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 1.

Aliasing.name: Aliasing
Aliasing.description: Artifact arising when the sampling interval violates the Nyquist criterion, causing high-frequency energy to fold back into the recorded spectrum. Temporal aliasing: dt > 1/(2*fmax). Spatial aliasing: dx > v/(2*fmax). Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 1.

ProcessingFlow.name: ProcessingFlow
ProcessingFlow.description: Ordered sequence of processing steps applied to raw seismic data to produce an imaged, interpretable seismic volume. Each step transforms the data in a specific way. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 1.

NoiseSuppressionFilter.name: NoiseSuppressionFilter
NoiseSuppressionFilter.description: Generic category of operations that attenuate coherent or incoherent noise in seismic data while preserving signal fidelity. Sub-types include DATVF, FXEdit and DenoiseFK. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 6.

DATVF.name: DATVF
DATVF.name_pt: DATVF — Denoising Adaptativo no Dominio T-V-F
DATVF.description: Data-Adaptive Time-Variant Frequency filter that applies a spatially and temporally varying bandpass to track signal frequency content changes with depth/time, suppressing noise outside the signal band without over-smoothing. Ref: Yilmaz 2001.

FXEdit.name: FXEdit
FXEdit.name_pt: FX Edit — Edicao no Dominio F-X
FXEdit.description: Coherent noise attenuation in the frequency-wavenumber (f-x) domain. Random noise appears as spatially uncorrelated; linear coherent noise is predicted and subtracted via Wiener prediction. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 6.

DenoiseFK.name: DenoiseFK
DenoiseFK.description: Frequency-wavenumber (f-k) fan filter that attenuates noise with apparent velocity outside the signal fan. Effective for ground-roll (slow, low-frequency) suppression in land data. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 6.

Deconvolution.name: Deconvolution
Deconvolution.description: Inverse filtering operation that compresses the source wavelet toward a spike (spiking) or reduces its length (predictive), thereby improving temporal resolution and attenuating multiples. Assumes convolutional model: x(t) = w(t)*r(t)+n(t). Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 2.

PredictiveDeconvolution.name: PredictiveDeconvolution
PredictiveDeconvolution.description: Deconvolution in which a prediction distance (lag) greater than one sample is used, concentrating the operation on multiple attenuation at a specified prediction lag (equal to the two-way travel time of the peg-leg multiple). Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 2.

SpikingDeconvolution.name: SpikingDeconvolution
SpikingDeconvolution.description: Wiener least-squares deconvolution with prediction lag=1 sample, aiming to collapse the wavelet to a spike and maximise temporal resolution. Assumes minimum-phase source wavelet and white reflectivity. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 2.

NMO.name: NMO
NMO.description: Normal Moveout correction: time-shift applied to each trace to remove the hyperbolic offset-dependent arrival time difference and align reflections before stacking. Requires RMS velocity model. Introduces NMO stretch at far offsets. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 3.

DMO.name: DMO
DMO.description: Dip Moveout correction applied after NMO to remove the dip-dependent component of moveout, transforming the data to zero-offset equivalent before stack. Precedes post-stack migration in conventional processing. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 4.

Stacking.name: Stacking
Stacking.description: Summation of NMO-corrected CMP traces to produce a single stacked trace with improved signal-to-noise ratio proportional to sqrt(fold). Pre-stack data are retained for AVO and inversion. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 3.

StaticCorrection.name: StaticCorrection
StaticCorrection.description: Time-shift applied to land seismic traces to compensate for near-surface weathering layer thickness and velocity variations, and for surface topography. Comprises elevation statics (field) and residual statics (processing). Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 3.

AmplitudeCompensation.name: AmplitudeCompensation
AmplitudeCompensation.description: Amplitude restoration to compensate for geometrical spreading loss and anelastic (Q) attenuation with depth, making amplitude proportional to reflectivity for AVO/DHI analysis. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 1.

SphericalDivergenceCorrection.name: SphericalDivergenceCorrection
SphericalDivergenceCorrection.description: Gain function proportional to t^2 * v^2(t) that compensates for amplitude decay due to spherical divergence of the wavefront. Applied early in the flow before deconvolution. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 1.

QCompensation.name: QCompensation
QCompensation.description: Inverse-Q filter that restores high-frequency amplitude and phase loss caused by anelastic attenuation (Q factor) during wave propagation. Critical in gas-saturated intervals. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 7.

Migration.name: Migration
Migration.description: Wave-equation-based imaging operator that collapses diffractions and repositions dipping reflectors to their true subsurface positions. Can be applied pre- or post-stack, in time or depth domain. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 4-5.

KirchhoffMigration.name: KirchhoffMigration
KirchhoffMigration.description: Asymptotic migration based on the Kirchhoff integral of the scalar wave equation. Computationally efficient for 3D pre-stack time and depth migration; less accurate than RTM in complex velocity structures. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 4.

RTM.name: RTM
RTM.description: Reverse Time Migration: two-way wave equation migration that propagates the source wavefield forward and receiver wavefield backward in time, applying an imaging condition. Handles complex velocity models (salt bodies) accurately. Computationally intensive. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 5.

SeismicInversion.name: SeismicInversion
SeismicInversion.description: Process of transforming seismic reflection data into quantitative rock property models (impedance, velocity, density) by solving the forward convolution model in reverse. Requires wavelet estimation and low-frequency model from wells. Ref: Russell 1988.

PostStackInversion.name: PostStackInversion
PostStackInversion.description: Inversion applied to stacked (zero-offset equivalent) seismic data to estimate acoustic impedance Ip = Vp * rho. Most mature and widely applied inversion method. Methods: band-limited, model-based, sparse-spike. Ref: Russell 1988.

PreStackInversion.name: PreStackInversion
PreStackInversion.description: Simultaneous inversion of partial-angle stacks to estimate Vp, Vs and density using the Zoeppritz equations or Aki-Richards linearisation. Enables direct Vp/Vs and elastic impedance extraction. Ref: Russell 1988; Connolly 1999.

AVOInversion.name: AVOInversion
AVOInversion.description: Amplitude Versus Offset inversion that extracts AVO intercept (A) and gradient (B) from CDP gathers using the Shuey two-term approximation, enabling fluid and lithology discrimination. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 11.

ElasticImpedanceInversion.name: ElasticImpedanceInversion
ElasticImpedanceInversion.description: Inversion of angle-dependent seismic data to Elastic Impedance (EI), the far-offset analogue of acoustic impedance. Defined as EI(theta) = Vp^(1+tan^2θ) * Vs^(-8*K*sin^2θ) * rho^(1-4*K*sin^2θ) where K=Vs^2/Vp^2. Ref: Connolly 1999.

FullWaveformInversion.name: FullWaveformInversion
FullWaveformInversion.name_pt: Inversao de Forma de Onda Completa (FWI)
FullWaveformInversion.description: Iterative inversion that minimises the misfit between observed and modelled waveforms to build high-resolution velocity and density models. Computationally intensive; requires low-frequency data (2-5 Hz). Ref: Yilmaz 2001.

AcousticImpedance.name: AcousticImpedance
AcousticImpedance.description: Product of P-wave velocity and bulk density: Ip = Vp * rho. Primary output of post-stack inversion. Units: g/cm3 * m/s. Used to calibrate well logs and identify lithology/fluid changes. Ref: Russell 1988.

ElasticImpedance.name: ElasticImpedance
ElasticImpedance.description: Angle-dependent impedance generalisation of acoustic impedance for non-zero incidence angle, enabling pre-stack seismic calibration. Defined by Connolly (1999). Units: varies with normalisation convention.

RockProperty.name: RockProperty
RockProperty.description: Quantitative petrophysical or rock-physics parameter derived from seismic inversion or well logs. Includes Vp/Vs ratio, lambda-rho and mu-rho for fluid and lithology discrimination. Ref: Russell 1988.

VpVsRatio.name: VpVsRatio
VpVsRatio.description: Ratio of P-wave to S-wave velocity. Sensitive to fluid saturation: brine-saturated rock Vp/Vs ~1.9-2.1; gas-saturated Vp/Vs ~1.4-1.7 (Gassmann fluid substitution). Derived from PreStackInversion. Ref: Russell 1988.

LambdaRho.name: LambdaRho
LambdaRho.description: Product of Lame parameter lambda and density: lambda*rho. Highly sensitive to pore fluid: gas decreases lambda-rho significantly relative to brine. Part of the lambda-mu-rho (LMR) rock-physics domain. Ref: Russell 1988.

MuRho.name: MuRho
MuRho.description: Product of shear modulus mu and density. Relatively insensitive to pore fluid but sensitive to lithology (shale vs sand). Pair with LambdaRho for fluid-lithology crossplot. Ref: Russell 1988.

SeismicAttribute.name: SeismicAttribute
SeismicAttribute.description: Quantitative measure derived from seismic data through mathematical transformation. Extracts information about structure, stratigraphy, fluid content or lithology not directly visible in the amplitude display. Ref: Chopra & Marfurt 2007.

Coherence.name: Coherence
Coherence.description: Measure of lateral similarity of seismic traces. Low coherence indicates faults, fractures or stratigraphic discontinuities. Original algorithm by Bahorich & Farmer (1995); enhanced by semblance (Marfurt et al. 1998). Ref: Chopra & Marfurt 2007.

Curvature.name: Curvature
Curvature.description: Second spatial derivative of the seismic horizon. Most-positive and most-negative curvature highlight flexures, collapse features and subtle faults. Sensitive to fracture corridors. Ref: Chopra & Marfurt 2007.

RMS.name: RMS
RMS.description: Root Mean Square amplitude computed over a time window. Sensitive to reflectivity strength; often brightens over hydrocarbons (Class III AVO). Ref: Chopra & Marfurt 2007.

SpectralDecomposition.name: SpectralDecomposition
SpectralDecomposition.description: Time-frequency analysis (short-window DFT, CWT or MP) that extracts amplitude and phase at discrete frequencies. Each frequency is sensitive to a specific bed thickness (tuning). Effective for stratigraphic thickness mapping. Ref: Chopra & Marfurt 2007.

InstantaneousPhase.name: InstantaneousPhase
InstantaneousPhase.description: Phase of the analytic signal derived via Hilbert transform. Highlights reflector continuity independent of amplitude strength. Useful for stratigraphic correlation. Ref: Chopra & Marfurt 2007.

Dip.name: Dip
Dip.description: Inline and crossline apparent dip of the seismic event derived from the gradient of the instantaneous phase or coherence scan. Input for dip-steered attribute computation. Ref: Chopra & Marfurt 2007.

Azimuth.name: Azimuth
Azimuth.description: Compass direction of the steepest dip of a reflector, derived jointly with the Dip attribute. Used to detect structural and fracture orientation. Ref: Chopra & Marfurt 2007.

DHI.name: DHI
DHI.description: Direct Hydrocarbon Indicator: seismic anomaly whose character is directly controlled by the presence of gas or oil in the reservoir. Includes bright spots, flat spots, phase reversals and AVO anomalies. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 11.

AVOAnomaly.name: AVOAnomaly
AVOAnomaly.description: Offset-dependent amplitude anomaly at a reservoir interface classified into four Rutherford-Williams classes (I-IV) based on normal-incidence impedance contrast and gradient sign. Class III (negative intercept, negative gradient) is the classic gas sand anomaly. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, ch. 11.

SOM.name: SOM
SOM.description: Self-Organising Map: unsupervised neural network that projects a multi-attribute seismic volume onto a 2D topology-preserving map for facies classification without prior labels. Ref: Coleou et al. 2003, The Leading Edge.

PCA.name: PCA
PCA.description: Principal Component Analysis applied to a multi-attribute seismic dataset to reduce dimensionality, decorrelate attributes and identify dominant seismic facies components before SOM or supervised classification. Ref: Coleou et al. 2003.

FaciesClassification.name: FaciesClassification
FaciesClassification.description: Assignment of seismic volumes or attribute maps to discrete facies classes that correlate with depositional environment or rock type. Can be supervised (with well labels) or unsupervised (SOM/PCA). Ref: Coleou et al. 2003; Chopra & Marfurt 2007.

SweetSpot.name: SweetSpot
SweetSpot.description: Geospatially bounded zone identified by the intersection of favourable seismic attributes (DHI, AVO anomaly, high porosity inversion) indicating an exploration or production target with elevated hydrocarbon potential. Ref: Chopra & Marfurt 2007.

samplingInterval.name: samplingInterval
samplingInterval.description: Time interval between consecutive recorded samples. Standard values: 1 ms (high-resolution), 2 ms (conventional), 4 ms (low-frequency). Must be dt <= 1/(2*fNyquist). Ref: Yilmaz 2001.

recordLength.name: recordLength
recordLength.description: Total duration of each recorded seismic trace, from shot instant to end of recording. Typical 6-10 s for deep targets.

fold.name: fold
fold.description: Number of independent seismic traces that contribute to a single stacked trace after CMP sorting. Higher fold improves signal-to-noise ratio. Standard 3D: 60-120x. Ref: Yilmaz 2001.

offset.name: offset
offset.description: Distance between source and receiver. Minimum offset determines shallow resolution; maximum offset determines depth penetration and NMO velocity resolution. Must be non-negative. Ref: Yilmaz 2001.

NyquistFrequency.name: NyquistFrequency
NyquistFrequency.description: Maximum alias-free frequency: fN = 1/(2*dt). For dt=2ms, fN=250Hz. Ref: Yilmaz 2001, eq. 1-1.

signalBandwidth.name: signalBandwidth
signalBandwidth.description: Frequency range of meaningful signal energy after instrument and earth filtering. Typical 8-80 Hz for conventional reflection seismics.

peakFrequency.name: peakFrequency
peakFrequency.description: Dominant frequency of the source wavelet or Ricker wavelet. Determines vertical resolution: lambda/4 = v/(4*fPeak). Ref: Yilmaz 2001.

operatingPressure.name: operatingPressure
operatingPressure.description: Working pressure of an airgun array at fire time. Typical value 138 bar (2000 psi). Controls peak-to-bubble ratio.

sweepFrequencyMin.name: sweepFrequencyMin
sweepFrequencyMin.description: Lowest frequency in the vibroseis pilot sweep.

sweepFrequencyMax.name: sweepFrequencyMax
sweepFrequencyMax.description: Highest frequency in the vibroseis pilot sweep.

sweepLength.name: sweepLength
sweepLength.description: Duration of the vibroseis sweep signal in seconds. Longer sweeps improve signal-to-noise ratio after correlation.

streamerLength.name: streamerLength
streamerLength.description: Total active length of a towed hydrophone cable. Controls maximum offset and hence depth penetration.

numberOfStreamers.name: numberOfStreamers
numberOfStreamers.description: Count of towed hydrophone cables. Increases cross-line coverage and survey efficiency.

gridSpacing.name: gridSpacing
gridSpacing.description: In-line and cross-line CMP bin dimensions. Determines lateral spatial aliasing limit: dx <= v/(2*fmax). Typical 12.5x12.5m to 25x25m in 3D surveys.

waterDepth.name: waterDepth
waterDepth.description: Water depth at survey location. Determines ghost frequency, node deployment logistics and cable operation window.

receiverSensitivity.name: receiverSensitivity
receiverSensitivity.description: Transduction efficiency of a receiver. For hydrophones: mV/Pa; for geophones: V/(m/s).

surveyArea.name: surveyArea
surveyArea.description: Total area covered by the seismic survey polygon.

processingStep.name: processingStep
processingStep.description: Ordered step in the processing flow, referencing the class of the operation applied.

stepOrder.name: stepOrder
stepOrder.description: Integer index of the processing step within the flow (1 = first).

operatorLength.name: operatorLength
operatorLength.description: Length of the deconvolution Wiener filter operator.

predictionLag.name: predictionLag
predictionLag.description: Prediction distance in predictive deconvolution, set to the expected peg-leg multiple TWTT.

velocityFieldType.name: velocityFieldType
velocityFieldType.description: Type of velocity model used in NMO or migration: RMS, interval, tomographic.

stretchMute.name: stretchMute
stretchMute.description: Maximum tolerable NMO stretch in percent; traces beyond this limit are muted to prevent low-frequency noise contamination of the stack.

qFactor.name: qFactor
qFactor.description: Quality factor: ratio of elastic energy stored to energy dissipated per cycle. Low Q (20-100) = high attenuation; high Q (200-1000) = low attenuation. Used in Q-compensation filter design.

migrationAlgorithm.name: migrationAlgorithm
migrationAlgorithm.description: Algorithm used in the migration step: Kirchhoff, RTM, FWI, Phase-Shift.

migrationDomain.name: migrationDomain
migrationDomain.description: Whether migration is performed in time or depth domain.

bandpassFilterLow.name: bandpassFilterLow
bandpassFilterLow.description: Low-frequency cut of a bandpass filter applied during processing.

bandpassFilterHigh.name: bandpassFilterHigh
bandpassFilterHigh.description: High-frequency cut of a bandpass filter applied during processing.

gainFunction.name: gainFunction
gainFunction.description: Mathematical form of the amplitude correction applied: t2v2, AGC, balance.

staticShift.name: staticShift
staticShift.description: Time shift in milliseconds applied to a trace by static correction.

acousticImpedance_value.name: acousticImpedance_value
acousticImpedance_value.description: Numerical value of acoustic impedance in g/cm3 * m/s. Typical sand: 6000-10000; shale: 7000-12000; carbonates: 12000-20000.

elasticImpedance_angle.name: elasticImpedance_angle
elasticImpedance_angle.description: Angle of incidence at which the elastic impedance is computed, typically 15, 30 or 45 degrees.

vpVsRatio_value.name: vpVsRatio_value
vpVsRatio_value.description: Numerical value of the Vp/Vs ratio. Gas: 1.4-1.7; brine: 1.9-2.1; carbonates: 1.7-2.0.

lambdaRho_value.name: lambdaRho_value
lambdaRho_value.description: lambda*rho in GPa*g/cm3. Decreases significantly in gas-saturated intervals.

muRho_value.name: muRho_value
muRho_value.description: mu*rho in GPa*g/cm3. Sensitive to rock framework; not to pore fluid.

avoIntercept.name: avoIntercept
avoIntercept.description: Zero-offset reflectivity coefficient A in the Shuey two-term AVO approximation R(theta) = A + B*sin^2(theta). Positive for hard kick, negative for soft kick.

avoGradient.name: avoGradient
avoGradient.description: AVO gradient B in the Shuey two-term approximation. Negative for Class III gas sands (bright spot). Ref: Yilmaz 2001.

avoClass.name: avoClass
avoClass.description: Rutherford-Williams AVO classification: I (+A, -B), II (-A or 0A, -B), IIp (+A, +B), III (-A, -B), IV (-A, +B). Ref: Yilmaz 2001.

coherenceValue.name: coherenceValue
coherenceValue.description: Numerical coherence or similarity value between 0 (discontinuous) and 1 (perfectly coherent).

curvatureType.name: curvatureType
curvatureType.description: Most-positive (k1), most-negative (k2), mean (km) or shape index curvature.

spectralFrequency.name: spectralFrequency
spectralFrequency.description: Frequency component extracted in spectral decomposition, corresponding to a specific tuning thickness.

somNeuronCount.name: somNeuronCount
somNeuronCount.description: Total number of neurons in the SOM grid (rows x cols). Controls classification resolution.

pcaVarianceExplained.name: pcaVarianceExplained
pcaVarianceExplained.description: Cumulative variance explained by the retained principal components, expressed as percentage. Typically 80-95% retained.

inversionMethod.name: inversionMethod
inversionMethod.description: Algorithm used in the inversion: band-limited, model-based, sparse-spike, simultaneous, FWI.

wellCount.name: wellCount
wellCount.description: Number of wells used to constrain the low-frequency model and wavelet extraction in the inversion.

uses_source.name: uses_source
uses_source.description: A SeismicSurvey uses one or more Source devices to generate seismic energy.

deploys_receiver.name: deploys_receiver
deploys_receiver.description: A SeismicSurvey deploys one or more Receiver types.

applies_geometry.name: applies_geometry
applies_geometry.description: A SeismicSurvey is characterised by an AcquisitionGeometry.

respects_nyquist.name: respects_nyquist
respects_nyquist.description: SamplingParameters respects the Nyquist criterion with respect to the signal bandwidth: samplingInterval <= 1/(2*signalBandwidthMax). Violation produces Aliasing.

characterised_by.name: characterised_by
characterised_by.description: A SeismicSurvey is characterised by SamplingParameters.

produces_aliasing.name: produces_aliasing
produces_aliasing.description: Violation of Nyquist constraint produces Aliasing artifact.

is_part_of_survey.name: is_part_of_survey
is_part_of_survey.description: AcquisitionGeometry or SamplingParameters is defined as part of a SeismicSurvey.

collocated_with.name: collocated_with
collocated_with.description: In OBC/OBN deployments a Hydrophone is collocated with a Geophone or AccelerometerMEMS at the same spatial position.

precedes.name: precedes
precedes.description: One processing step temporally precedes another in the processing flow.

part_of_flow.name: part_of_flow
part_of_flow.description: A processing step is part of a ProcessingFlow.

uses_deconvolution.name: uses_deconvolution
uses_deconvolution.description: A ProcessingFlow applies a Deconvolution step.

uses_migration.name: uses_migration
uses_migration.description: A ProcessingFlow applies a Migration algorithm.

compensates_amplitude.name: compensates_amplitude
compensates_amplitude.description: A ProcessingFlow applies an AmplitudeCompensation step.

derived_from.name: derived_from
derived_from.description: A RockProperty or SeismicAttribute is mathematically derived from SeismicInversion output or another attribute.

correlates_with.name: correlates_with
correlates_with.description: A SeismicAttribute correlates with a RockProperty or another SeismicAttribute in a statistical or physical sense.

indicates.name: indicates
indicates.description: A DHI or AVOAnomaly indicates the potential presence of a hydrocarbon accumulation or specific lithofacies.

classified_by.name: classified_by
classified_by.description: A seismic facies volume is classified by FaciesClassification using SOM or PCA.

uses_pca.name: uses_pca
uses_pca.description: A FaciesClassification or SOM uses PCA for dimensionality reduction of seismic attributes.

input_to_inversion.name: input_to_inversion
input_to_inversion.description: Processed seismic data or partial stack is input to a SeismicInversion.

yields.name: yields
yields.description: A SeismicInversion yields a RockProperty volume as output.

combined_in.name: combined_in
combined_in.description: Multiple SeismicAttributes are combined in a FaciesClassification or SweetSpot delineation.

constrained_by_well.name: constrained_by_well
constrained_by_well.description: SeismicInversion is constrained by well log data for low-frequency model and wavelet extraction.

I_BUZ3D_2022.name: Buzios 3D Wide-Azimuth Streamer Survey 2022
I_BUZ3D_2022.description: Broadband wide-azimuth 3D towed-streamer survey over the Buzios carbonate pre-salt field, Santos Basin, offshore Brazil. 14 streamers x 8 km, 100 m cable separation, 6.25 m group interval, 2 ms sample interval.
I_BUZ3D_2022.source: Analogous to Buzios-field 3D acquisition programs described in Petrobras technical literature

I_LAND_VIB_2021.name: Parnaiba Basin Vibroseis Land Survey 2021
I_LAND_VIB_2021.name_pt: Levantamento Terrestre Vibrosseis Bacia do Parnaiba 2021
I_LAND_VIB_2021.description: Orthogonal-template 3D vibroseis survey in the Parnaiba Basin, northeastern Brazil. Four vibrator fleet, 4-8 Hz linear sweep, 16 s sweep length, 2 ms sample interval, maximum offset 6000 m.
I_LAND_VIB_2021.source: Representative of ANP-regulated onshore 3D programs in Brazilian interior basins

I_OBN_LULA_2023.name: Lula OBN 4C Full-Azimuth Survey 2023
I_OBN_LULA_2023.description: Ocean-Bottom Node full-azimuth 4C survey over the Lula giant pre-salt field, Santos Basin. Nodes deployed at 2100 m water depth on 400 x 400 m grid; P- and S-wave data recorded for joint PP/PS inversion and imaging through the salt canopy.
I_OBN_LULA_2023.source: Analogous to Petrobras OBN programs in Santos Basin ultra-deep water

PF_MARINE_RTM.name: Deep-Water Pre-Stack RTM Processing Flow
PF_MARINE_RTM.description: Typical pre-stack depth migration processing flow for deep-water broadband 3D marine data: demultiple, deconvolution, NMO/velocity analysis, SRME, RTM.
PF_MARINE_RTM.source: Representative of Santos Basin pre-salt processing workflows (Yilmaz 2001)

PF_LAND_KIRCHHOFF.name: Land Vibroseis Kirchhoff Time Migration Flow
PF_LAND_KIRCHHOFF.name_pt: Fluxo de Migracao de Kirchhoff em Tempo — Vibrosseis Terrestre
PF_LAND_KIRCHHOFF.description: Standard processing flow for land vibroseis 3D: cross-correlation, statics, bandpass filter, decon, NMO, DMO, Kirchhoff time migration.

PF_OBN_PS.name: OBN 4C PP/PS Joint Processing Flow
PF_OBN_PS.name_pt: Fluxo de Processamento Conjunto PP/PS OBN 4C
PF_OBN_PS.description: Processing flow for OBN 4C ocean-bottom data including dual-sensor summation, P- and S-wave separation, PP/PS registration, NMO for both modes and joint inversion preparation.
